risk.desc: Potential thermal stress due to rapid temperature changes in LEO.
risk.mit: Implement thermal management strategies such as thermal coatings and heat sinks.

risk.desc: Risk of structural failure under launch vibration.
risk.mit: Conduct extensive structural analysis and testing to ensure compliance with launch vibration requirements.

risk.desc: Risk of electrical interference between boards.
risk.mit: Ensure proper electrical isolation using PC/104 standoffs and shielding.

risk.desc: Risk of mechanical interface failure during deployment.
risk.mit: Perform thorough mechanical interface testing and validation.

risk.desc: Risk of access panel failure during field serviceability.
risk.mit: Design access panels with robust locking mechanisms and clear labeling.

risk.desc: The deployment mechanism may not be robust enough for the expected launch vibration loads.
risk.mit: Conduct vibration testing and refine the design as necessary.

risk.desc: The solar panel face may not withstand the thermal cycling in LEO.
risk.mit: Implement thermal management measures such as thermal coatings or heat pipes.

risk.desc: The separation system may fail if the burn-wire release is not properly calibrated.
risk.mit: Perform thorough testing of the burn-wire release mechanism.

risk.desc: The hinge mechanism may not provide sufficient damping during deployment.
risk.mit: Include a deployment damper in the design and test its effectiveness.

risk.desc: The service access panel may not provide adequate access for maintenance.
risk.mit: Review the size and location of the service access panel to ensure it meets requirements.

generated_parts.label: Antenna Deployer x 2
generated_parts.spec: Tape-spring UHF/S-band antenna deployer, stowed flat, burn-wire release, latching tab

requirements: Deploy tape-spring UHF/S-band antenna, stowed flat, burn-wire release, latching tab
Antenna slot, radio board slot, RF connector path
Battery, sensor, actuator, cartridge, payload, cable, or joint modules remain removable
Harnesses, tubes, or service channels are routed through visible raceways or protected channels
Access is represented by geometry: hatch, pocket, channel, fastener pattern, slot, window, or removable cover
Every cable exit shows strain relief geometry (clamp, grommet, tie point) and routing respects a stated minimum bend radius away from moving joints and sharp edges
Connectors are reachable without disassembling structure, have finger/tool clearance, and mention keying or labeling to prevent mis-mating
Motor power lines and sensor/encoder signal lines run in separate channels or are spaced/crossed at right angles
Each electronics module states its grounding scheme (star point, chassis bond, isolated) and signal returns route alongside their signals
PCBs mount on standoffs with keep-out clearance to walls and moving parts, connector edges face access openings, and mounting holes match standard board patterns
P0 internal_feature_targets appear as part_tree children and geometry_ops ids or targets
Most geometry_ops include args.at coordinates distributed across the local subsystem volume
Uses drill, pocket, boss, channel, pattern_polar, fillet, chamfer
Every part_tree child has 4+ dedicated geometry_ops (body+subtract+interface+finish)
A primary datum feature (flat, bore, or pin) is explicitly named and placed
Adjacent interfaces include fit_class (H7/h6, LC2) and stack_up_mm
cad_brief includes ordered assembly_sequence with tool, torque, datum per step
Uses seed-characteristic ops: chamfer, boss, drill, subtract
Dimensions match cubesat scale: 100x100x340mm (3U CubeSat). Wall thickness, hole diameters, fillet radii are physically plausible
